title.en: The Great Liberation through Hearing in the Bardos

source.attribution: A treasure revealed by Guru Padmasambhava

blocks.en: The Great Liberation through Hearing in the Bardos
Part One — The Bardo of Dying and the Dawning of the Peaceful Deities

How to read this text.  The shaded passages marked ◆ READ ALOUD are the words spoken directly to the dying or deceased person. Everything else is guidance for the reader and is not spoken aloud. Tibetan is given above each English passage.

Herein is contained the Great Liberation through Hearing — an illumination of the bardo of reality — extracted from The Peaceful and Wrathful Deities: the Profound Dharma of Self-Liberation through Enlightened Intention.

This Great Liberation through Hearing — the skillful means by which yogins of middling capacity are liberated within the bardos — has three parts: the preliminaries, the main practice, and the conclusion.

First, liberation for those at the stage of the preliminaries. One takes the graded guidance into experience; those of the sharpest faculties are liberated for certain by that guidance alone — through The Pointing Out of Awareness: Natural Liberation through Naked Seeing (Chapter 4).

If liberation does not come thereby, then in the bardo of dying the yogin applies The Transference of Consciousness: Natural Liberation through Recollection (phowa, Chapter 10). Yogins of middling capacity are certainly liberated by that.

If liberation still does not occur, then in the bardo of reality apply this Great Liberation through Hearing with diligence. First, the yogin watches for the signs of certain death, examining them as in the mirror of Natural Liberation through the Recognition of the Signs and Marks of Death — the Mirror that Lays Bare the Visible Signs of Death. When the signs of certain death are complete, apply the transference. If it takes hold, there is no need to read the Liberation through Hearing aloud.

But if the transference does not take hold, read this Liberation through Hearing aloud beside the corpse, with clear sound and distinct words. If the body is not present, sit at the bed or seat of the deceased, proclaim the power of truth, summon the consciousness of the dead, and — imagining them present and listening before you — read it aloud.
At this time, relatives and dear friends should be held back from weeping and loud lament, for such display is not fitting now.

If the body is present, then in the interval just after the outer breath has ceased, a spiritual teacher — or a vajra-sibling, a devoted practitioner, or a like-minded friend — places the lips close to the ear, without quite touching it, and reads this Great Liberation through Hearing aloud.

Now, the actual exposition of the Liberation through Hearing. First, make vast offerings to the Three Jewels. If these cannot be gathered, arrange whatever supports for visualization you have, and present a measureless cloud of offerings emanated in the mind.

Then recite, three or seven times, the Aspiration Calling upon the Buddhas and Bodhisattvas for Aid. After that, chant melodiously, close to the ear: the Aspiration that Protects from the Fear of the Bardos; the Aspiration that Delivers from the Perilous Straits of the Bardos; and the Root Verses of the Six Bardos.
Thereafter, read this Great Liberation through Hearing aloud seven times, or three, or as often as the occasion allows.

The main practice has three parts: the pointing out of clear light in the bardo of dying; the great pointing out that introduces the bardo of reality; and the teaching on closing the womb-door in the bardo of becoming.

heading.en: i.  Pointing Out the Clear Light in the Bardo of Dying

blocks.en: First, the pointing out of clear light in the bardo of dying. This is given for those who understand well yet have not been set face to face with clear light; for those who have been set face to face but have little familiarity; and for all ordinary beings who have received little to none experiential guidance.
By it, such beings recognize the ground clear light and — with no bardo intervening — attain the unborn dharmakāya, rising straight upward, all-penetrating.
Pointing Out the Clear Light of the Ground

As to how the pointing out is made: best of all is for the root teacher from whom the dying one received guidance to be present. If the teacher cannot come, then a vajra-sibling of one and the same samaya; and if none such, a spiritual friend who holds the same lineage should attend.

If none of these can come, then someone who can read with clear sound and distinct words should recite the pointing out many times over. Thereby the dying one recalls what the teacher formerly pointed out and, set face to face with the ground clear light, is liberated beyond all doubt.

As for the time of the pointing out: once the outer breath has ceased, the wind dissolves into the central channel of primal awareness, and consciousness dawns — vivid and bare — as clear light free of all elaboration.
Then the wind reverses and slips out into the right and left channels, and the appearances of the bardo dawn all at once.

So the pointing out must be made before the wind has escaped into the right and left channels. In general, the inner breath remains within the central channel for about the time it takes to eat a meal.
As for the manner of the pointing out: best is if the transference of consciousness is accomplished just as the breath is about to cease. If it is not, speak these words:

O Child of the Noble Family — (say the name) — rest now in that very state. The time has come to seek the path. The instant your breath stops, what your teacher pointed out to you before will dawn — the ground clear light of the first bardo. As the outer breath ceases, all things grow empty, utterly bare, like open space; and a naked rigpa dawns — not something apart from you — radiant and empty, without edge or center. In that moment, recognize your own nature, and rest in that very state. I too will set you face to face with it then.
Speak these words into the ear many times before the breath ceases, pressing them into the mind of the dying one.

Then, when the breath is on the very point of ceasing, lay the dying person on the right side in the lion's posture and take the pulse. Once the throbbing of the two arteries of the throat has stopped, press them firmly at the points on the neck.
Then the wind, having entered the central channel, cannot turn back, and it is sure to rise out through the crown aperture. The pointing out continues here too.
This phase is also called the clear light of reality of the first bardo. It is the one unmistaken awakened intention of the dharmakāya, dawning in the mindstream of every being.

At death, this is the interval after the outer breath has ceased and before the inner breath has ceased, while the wind dissolves into the central channel (avadhūtī). Ordinary people call this state ‘fainting.’

Its duration is uncertain, for it depends on the soundness of the constitution and on one's stage in the practice of channels and winds. For those with some real experience, those steady in calm abiding, or those whose channels are sound, it can last a long while.
Persevere in the pointing out, repeating the instruction again and again, until serous fluid appears at the openings of the sense-faculties.
For those of great evil or of unsound channels, it may last no longer than a single snap of the fingers. For others, as long as it takes to eat a meal.
Since most sūtras and tantras say the swoon at death lasts three and a half days, one should generally persevere in this pointing out of clear light for that length of time.

As for the manner of pointing out: if able, the dying one should set themselves free by [the virtue of their own practice], by their own power. If unable, then a teacher, a student, or a vajra-sibling who was a close friend should stay near.

Clearly name the signs in their sequence — ‘now the sign of earth dissolving into water is present,’ and so on, as set down in Natural Liberation through the Recognition of the Signs and Marks of Death. Then, as the sequence of signs nears completion, rouse in the dying one this awakening of the heart, beginning:
Address them as ‘O Child of the Noble Family’; or, if the dying one is a teacher, say gently and softly into the ear,
‘O Venerable One, I pray you — do not stray from rousing the awakened heart.’
To a vajra-sibling or anyone else, call them by name and say:

O Child of the Noble Family, death is now coming. Rouse your heart in this way: ‘Alas — the hour of my death has come. So now, taking this very death as my support, with love and compassion and the awakened heart alone I set my mind: for the sake of all beings, as limitless as space, I will attain perfect buddhahood.’ And in particular think: ‘For the sake of all beings, here and now I will recognize this dying as the clear light, the dharmakāya; and within that state I will win the supreme attainment of mahāmudrā, and so work for the good of all. And if I do not accomplish it, then I will recognize the bardo for what it is, and there make manifest the mahāmudrā of union — the body that joins the two — and, appearing in whatever form will tame each being, I will work for the welfare of all beings, as limitless as the reaches of space.’  Without parting from this aspiration of the awakened heart, recall now the practice of the instructions you grew familiar with before.

Speak these words clearly, lips at the ear. Not letting the dying one's attention stray even for an instant, make the practice vivid for them. Then, when the outer breath has wholly ceased and the two arteries have been pressed firmly, point out clearly with these words:
If the person was a teacher, or a spiritual friend greater than oneself, say:
Venerable One, the ground clear light is now dawning before you. Recognize it, and rest directly in its experience.
Thus one addresses a teacher. To everyone else, point out with these words:
O Child of the Noble Family — (say the name) — listen! Pure clear light, reality itself, is now dawning before you. Recognize it!

O Child of the Noble Family: this present awareness of yours — its very essence vivid and clear, ungraspable as any thing, mark, or color, standing wide open as sheer emptiness — this itself is dharmatā, the All-Good Mother, Samantabhadrī.

The essence of this awareness of yours is empty — yet not lapsed into a blank, annihilated emptiness; for your awareness, unceasing, is lucid, vivid, sparkling. This very vividness is the Buddha Samantabhadra, the All-Good.
These two — the essence of your own rigpa, empty and established as no thing at all, and your awareness, vividly and radiantly present — abide utterly indivisible: and this itself is the dharmakāya.
This rigpa of yours — clarity and emptiness inseparable — abides as a vast mass of light; unborn and undying, it is the very Buddha of Changeless Light. Simply to recognize this is enough.
If you recognize this vivid essence of your own awareness as buddhahood, then to look into your own rigpa is itself to rest in the awakened intention of all the buddhas.
Recite this three times, or seven, with clear sound and distinct words.

By this, first, the dying one recalls what the teacher formerly pointed out; second, they are set face to face with their own naked rigpa as clear light; and third, recognizing it, they become the dharmakāya, beyond meeting and parting, and are liberated for certain. Thus comes recognition of the first clear light — the clear light of the ground.
Pointing Out the Clear Light of the Path

If it is feared the first clear light was not recognized, then what is called the second clear light dawns. This too is said to come a little after the time it takes to eat a meal, once the outer breath has ceased.

According to good or evil deeds, the wind escapes into either the right or the left channel and emerges through one of the body's gates, and consciousness dawns, suddenly lucid. Though this too is said to last about the time of a meal, it depends, as before, on whether the channels are sound and whether one has meditative experience.

At this point, consciousness has come forth from the body, and at first one does not know whether one has died or not. One still sees one's relatives as before and hears their cries of grief. Here — before the fierce, bewildering appearances of karma have arisen, and before the terrors of Yama have come — the oral guidance should be given.

This guidance concerns both the completion stage and the generation stage. For a completion-stage practitioner, call them three times by name and repeat the pointing out of clear light given above. For a generation-stage practitioner, recite the sādhana-framework of whatever deity is their own, saying:

O Child of the Noble Family, meditate upon this, your yidam. Do not be distracted! Fix your longing intently upon the deity. Meditate on it as apparent yet without inherent nature — like the moon in water. Do not meditate on it as something solid and material.
Say this clearly. And if the person is an ordinary one without practice, point out simply: ‘Meditate on the Lord of Great Compassion!’
By such pointing out, even those who could not recognize the bardo will recognize it, beyond all doubt.
For although the teacher pointed this out during life, those of little familiarity cannot make it vivid by themselves in the bardo; a teacher or vajra-sibling must surely make it clear for them.

And for those who, though familiar with the path, are swayed by violent pain at death and cannot hold to mindfulness, this instruction is essential. So too for those who, despite former familiarity, have let their vows lapse or broken their root samaya and are bound for the lower realms — for them, above all, it is indispensable.
It is best if recognition comes in the first bardo. But if it does not, then by making it vivid in the second bardo, potential (Gotra) is awakened and liberation attained.
This second bardo is called the ‘impure illusory body.’ In it, a moment of lucidity comes, though the dead one does not know whether they are dead or not.

If the guidance takes hold now, the mother and child of reality meet, and one is no longer turned by the power of karma. Just as sunlight dispels darkness, so the clear light of the path overpowers the force of karma, and liberation is won.
This so-called second bardo is the sudden dawning of the mental body; consciousness goes on circling in a daze, as before. If the oral guidance is given at this time, its purpose is accomplished

Since the bewildering appearances generated by past actions have not yet arisen, the deceased is able to direct his or her attention anywhere. Therefore, even though the individual may not have attained recognition of the clear light of the ground, they will be liberated by the recognition of the clear light of the path, which is the second intermediate state.

If liberation is still not attained through the above introductions, then the Intermediate State of Reality, also called 'the third intermediate state', will arise. It is during this third phase that the bewildering apparitions, which are the products of past actions, emerge. Therefore, it is extremely important to read aloud the following Great **Introduction to the Intermediate State of Reality** at this juncture. It is most powerful and beneficial.
